The Wikidata data explicitly lists 'follows: Varian Associates', indicating that they are distinct entities in a succession chain. Varian Associates (the employer on the card) was the parent company before it spun off its medical, semiconductor, and instrument businesses into separate corporations in 1999.

results.possibleIdentity: Harvey J. Hindin was a microwave engineer, technical editor, and author active from the 1960s through the 1990s. In the late 1960s, he worked as an engineer for the LEL Division of Varian Associates, located in Copiague and Deer Park, New York. During this period, he specialized in microwave technology, publishing several significant papers in the IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques on subjects such as directional couplers, scattering parameters, and network synthesis. He is also noted for his interest in recreational mathematics, contributing to Martin Gardner's 'Mathematical Games' column in Scientific American on the topic of 'additive persistence.' By the early 1980s, Hindin transitioned into technical journalism, serving as the Communications & Microwave editor for Electronics magazine (McGraw-Hill) and later as a senior editor for Computer Design magazine. In 1985, he co-authored 'Artificial Intelligence in Business, Science, and Industry: Fundamentals' with Wendy B. Rauch-Hindin. In his later career, he worked as an industry analyst for D.H. Brown Associates. He was also known for his involvement in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which fostered collaboration between the technical and artistic communities.
